title: Pyelonephritis — Acute
oneLiner: Acute bacterial renal infection from ascending UTI; causes sepsis and AKI; treat aggressively with IV antibiotics.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic shock (urosepsis):** Hypotension (SBP <90 or MAP <65) + lactate ≥2 mmol/L despite initial fluid bolus → immediate antibiotics, repeat bolus, involve sepsis team/ICU.
- **Obstructed pyelonephritis:** Unilateral CVA tenderness + fever + hydronephrosis on bedside USS → needs urgent decompression (nephrostomy) by urology; do not delay antibiotics.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fever (≥38°C) and rigors
  - Unilateral flank or loin pain (constant)
  - Dysuria, frequency, urgency
  - Nausea/vomiting (common, not universal)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness
  - Tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypotension (if septic)
  - Suprapubic tenderness may be present (if cystitis component)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Lower UTI (cystitis)** – no CVA tenderness, temperature <38°C, urine dip positive but flank pain absent.
- **Renal calculi** – colicky pain, no fever unless superimposed infection; haematuria common.
- **Acute cholecystitis / appendicitis** – right-sided pain, Murphy’s sign or RIF tenderness; urine dip negative.
- **Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)** – lower abdominal/cervical motion tenderness, vaginal discharge.
- **Splitting feature:** Urinary symptoms + unilateral CVA tenderness + pyuria = pyelonephritis over abdominal sources.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dipstick: nitrites, leukocytes, blood
  - Urine culture (mid-stream or catheter sample) before antibiotics
  - Pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age)
  - Bedside USS: hydronephrosis, abscess
  - Lactate (venous or arterial)
  - Blood cultures (two sets, preferably before antibiotics)
  - First-hour sepsis screen if NEWS2 ≥5.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, blood cultures, lactate
  - Consider calcium, magnesium (if AKI suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Renal tract USS (within 24h if high risk / no response to treatment)
  - **Second-line (if complicated or no improvement):** CT KUB (non-contrast) to detect stones, abscess, obstruction
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Clinical diagnosis: fever + flank pain + pyuria (≥10 WBC/hpf) ± positive culture.
  - No single scoring system widely used; severity judged by SIRS/qSOFA/NEWS2.
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood cultures and urine culture before first antibiotic dose** — omission delays de-escalation and misses antibiotic resistance.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Perform sepsis six bundle: oxygen, blood cultures, IV antibiotics, IV fluids, lactate, monitor urine output.
  - IV antibiotics (see Drugs section)
  - IV fluid bolus: 500 mL–1 L Hartmann’s or 0.9% sodium chloride if hypotensive or lactate >2.
  - Catheterise if high-output or to monitor urine output (not routine).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics until afebrile and clinically improving (usually 24–48 h).
  - Switch to oral antibiotics when afebrile for ≥24 h and tolerating oral intake (see Drugs section).
  - Maintain hydration (IV fluids if oral intake poor) — 1.5–2 L per 24 h, adjust for AKI.
  - Reassess daily: temperature, pain, urine output, NEWS2.
  - If no improvement by 48 h → repeat imaging (USS or CT) and review cultures.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete oral course: 7–14 days total (complicated: 10–14 days; uncomplicated: 7 days).
  - Consider urine culture at end of treatment if recurrent UTI.
  - Address predisposing factors: diabetes, stones, pregnancy, catheter, structural abnormality.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann’s at 1 L/8 h (adjust for AKI, heart failure, elderly).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (or 20 mg if CrCl <30 or weight <50 kg) [check local policy].
  - **Diet/NBM:** Allow oral intake as tolerated; NBM only if vomiting or impending procedure.
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 (increase to 1-hourly if NEWS ≥5 or unstable).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (IV initial empiric):**
  - **Co-amoxiclav** 1.2 g IV TDS (1 h infusion over 30 min preferred) — use if no penicillin allergy.
  - **Ceftriaxone** 1 g IV OD (for non-severe penicillin allergy) — give over 15–30 min.
  - **Gentamicin** [check local policy] — usual dose 5–7 mg/kg IV once daily (monitor levels: trough <1 mg/L at 24 h). Preferred if severe sepsis/septic shock. **Always check baseline renal function.**
- **Oral stepdown:**
  - **Co-amoxiclav** 625 mg PO TDS (if susceptible)
  - **Ciprofloxacin** 500 mg PO BD (if resistance pattern allows; avoid overuse)
  - **Trimethoprim** 200 mg PO BD (if culture sensitive)
  - Duration: 7 days (uncomplicated) or 10–14 days (complicated: obstruction, male, pregnancy, elderly, renal transplant).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: penicillin allergy (avoid; give ceftriaxone or gentamicin).
  - Ciprofloxacin: avoid in pregnancy, children; risk of tendon rupture in elderly.
  - Gentamicin: nephrotoxic + ototoxic; avoid prolonged use >5 days without review.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: reduce frequency in severe renal impairment (e.g., CrCl <30 → 1.2 g IV BD, then reduce further).
  - Gentamicin: dose by actual body weight; check levels before second dose; adjust interval in renal impairment.
  - Ceftriaxone: no dose adjustment required in renal impairment (but caution in severe hepatic/renal failure).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin — failure to check serum levels before second dose** → risk of nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity. **Also: using co-amoxiclav in penicillin anaphylaxis** → risk of fatal reaction.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Septic shock / acute kidney injury** (common, monitor urine output and creatinine daily)
  - **Renal abscess** (persistent fever >72 h despite antibiotics)
  - **Emphysematous pyelonephritis** (gas in renal parenchyma – mainly diabetics; needs urgent nephrectomy)
  - **Papillary necrosis** (diabetics, sickle cell – haematuria, sloughing tissue in urine)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Renal scarring** → hypertension, chronic kidney disease (especially in children, pregnant women)
  - **Recurrent infections** (investigate for stones, reflux, obstruction)
sections.Prognosis:
- Uncomplicated pyelonephritis in young women: >95% cure with 7-day antibiotics.
- Mortality <1% overall, but rises to 10–30% in septic shock / elderly / immunocompromised.
- Clearance of bacteriuria usually within 48–72 h of effective therapy.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check pregnancy test before imaging (CT) or prescribing ciprofloxacin >> do not expose fetus.**
- **If no response to antibiotics by 48 h, repeat imaging (CT KUB) to look for abscess, stone, obstruction, or emphysematous changes.**
- **Obtain two sets of blood cultures before antibiotics — this is the only way to detect ESBL/MRSA organisms and escalate appropriately.**
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not rely on urine dipstick alone if patient has signs of sepsis** — start empiric antibiotics immediately.
- **Do not discharge a febrile patient with pyelonephritis on oral antibiotics without 24 h afebrile period** — risk of early relapse.
- **Do not forget to check gentamicin levels (trough and peak if using once-daily) and adjust dose or interval** — preventable nephrotoxicity.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Fever not settling after 48 h of oral antibiotics
  - Worsening flank pain, rigors, confusion, or reduced urine output
  - Unable to tolerate oral fluids or medication (vomiting)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 48–72 h for clinical check
  - Repeat urine culture 7 days after finishing antibiotics if recurrent infection or pregnancy
  - Outpatient CT KUB if first episode in male, recurrent, or complicated (arrange before discharge)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥5** → review by med reg/ICU outreach; consider critical care transfer.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L after initial fluid bolus** → escalate to ICU for vasopressors and source control.
- **MAP <65 mmHg despite 1L crystalloid** → involve ICU; consider noradrenaline.
- **Anuria or acute renal failure (creatinine rise >100% from baseline) + flank pain/proven obstruction** → urgent urology for decompression (nephrostomy or stent).

rawMarkdown:
## Pyelonephritis — Acute
**One-liner:** Acute bacterial renal infection from ascending UTI; causes sepsis and AKI; treat aggressively with IV antibiotics.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic shock (urosepsis):** Hypotension (SBP <90 or MAP <65) + lactate ≥2 mmol/L despite initial fluid bolus → immediate antibiotics, repeat bolus, involve sepsis team/ICU.
- **Obstructed pyelonephritis:** Unilateral CVA tenderness + fever + hydronephrosis on bedside USS → needs urgent decompression (nephrostomy) by urology; do not delay antibiotics.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fever (≥38°C) and rigors
  - Unilateral flank or loin pain (constant)
  - Dysuria, frequency, urgency
  - Nausea/vomiting (common, not universal)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness
  - Tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypotension (if septic)
  - Suprapubic tenderness may be present (if cystitis component)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Lower UTI (cystitis)** – no CVA tenderness, temperature <38°C, urine dip positive but flank pain absent.
- **Renal calculi** – colicky pain, no fever unless superimposed infection; haematuria common.
- **Acute cholecystitis / appendicitis** – right-sided pain, Murphy’s sign or RIF tenderness; urine dip negative.
- **Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID)** – lower abdominal/cervical motion tenderness, vaginal discharge.
- **Splitting feature:** Urinary symptoms + unilateral CVA tenderness + pyuria = pyelonephritis over abdominal sources.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dipstick: nitrites, leukocytes, blood
  - Urine culture (mid-stream or catheter sample) before antibiotics
  - Pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age)
  - Bedside USS: hydronephrosis, abscess
  - Lactate (venous or arterial)
  - Blood cultures (two sets, preferably before antibiotics)
  - First-hour sepsis screen if NEWS2 ≥5.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, blood cultures, lactate
  - Consider calcium, magnesium (if AKI suspected)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Renal tract USS (within 24h if high risk / no response to treatment)
  - **Second-line (if complicated or no improvement):** CT KUB (non-contrast) to detect stones, abscess, obstruction
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Clinical diagnosis: fever + flank pain + pyuria (≥10 WBC/hpf) ± positive culture.
  - No single scoring system widely used; severity judged by SIRS/qSOFA/NEWS2.
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood cultures and urine culture before first antibiotic dose** — omission delays de-escalation and misses antibiotic resistance.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Perform sepsis six bundle: oxygen, blood cultures, IV antibiotics, IV fluids, lactate, monitor urine output.
  - IV antibiotics (see Drugs section)
  - IV fluid bolus: 500 mL–1 L Hartmann’s or 0.9% sodium chloride if hypotensive or lactate >2.
  - Catheterise if high-output or to monitor urine output (not routine).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics until afebrile and clinically improving (usually 24–48 h).
  - Switch to oral antibiotics when afebrile for ≥24 h and tolerating oral intake (see Drugs section).
  - Maintain hydration (IV fluids if oral intake poor) — 1.5–2 L per 24 h, adjust for AKI.
  - Reassess daily: temperature, pain, urine output, NEWS2.
  - If no improvement by 48 h → repeat imaging (USS or CT) and review cultures.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete oral course: 7–14 days total (complicated: 10–14 days; uncomplicated: 7 days).
  - Consider urine culture at end of treatment if recurrent UTI.
  - Address predisposing factors: diabetes, stones, pregnancy, catheter, structural abnormality.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann’s at 1 L/8 h (adjust for AKI, heart failure, elderly).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (or 20 mg if CrCl <30 or weight <50 kg) [check local policy].
  - **Diet/NBM:** Allow oral intake as tolerated; NBM only if vomiting or impending procedure.
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 (increase to 1-hourly if NEWS ≥5 or unstable).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (IV initial empiric):**
  - **Co-amoxiclav** 1.2 g IV TDS (1 h infusion over 30 min preferred) — use if no penicillin allergy.
  - **Ceftriaxone** 1 g IV OD (for non-severe penicillin allergy) — give over 15–30 min.
  - **Gentamicin** [check local policy] — usual dose 5–7 mg/kg IV once daily (monitor levels: trough <1 mg/L at 24 h). Preferred if severe sepsis/septic shock. **Always check baseline renal function.**
- **Oral stepdown:**
  - **Co-amoxiclav** 625 mg PO TDS (if susceptible)
  - **Ciprofloxacin** 500 mg PO BD (if resistance pattern allows; avoid overuse)
  - **Trimethoprim** 200 mg PO BD (if culture sensitive)
  - Duration: 7 days (uncomplicated) or 10–14 days (complicated: obstruction, male, pregnancy, elderly, renal transplant).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: penicillin allergy (avoid; give ceftriaxone or gentamicin).
  - Ciprofloxacin: avoid in pregnancy, children; risk of tendon rupture in elderly.
  - Gentamicin: nephrotoxic + ototoxic; avoid prolonged use >5 days without review.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: reduce frequency in severe renal impairment (e.g., CrCl <30 → 1.2 g IV BD, then reduce further).
  - Gentamicin: dose by actual body weight; check levels before second dose; adjust interval in renal impairment.
  - Ceftriaxone: no dose adjustment required in renal impairment (but caution in severe hepatic/renal failure).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin — failure to check serum levels before second dose** → risk of nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity. **Also: using co-amoxiclav in penicillin anaphylaxis** → risk of fatal reaction.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Septic shock / acute kidney injury** (common, monitor urine output and creatinine daily)
  - **Renal abscess** (persistent fever >72 h despite antibiotics)
  - **Emphysematous pyelonephritis** (gas in renal parenchyma – mainly diabetics; needs urgent nephrectomy)
  - **Papillary necrosis** (diabetics, sickle cell – haematuria, sloughing tissue in urine)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Renal scarring** → hypertension, chronic kidney disease (especially in children, pregnant women)
  - **Recurrent infections** (investigate for stones, reflux, obstruction)

### Prognosis
- Uncomplicated pyelonephritis in young women: >95% cure with 7-day antibiotics.
- Mortality <1% overall, but rises to 10–30% in septic shock / elderly / immunocompromised.
- Clearance of bacteriuria usually within 48–72 h of effective therapy.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check pregnancy test before imaging (CT) or prescribing ciprofloxacin >> do not expose fetus.**
- **If no response to antibiotics by 48 h, repeat imaging (CT KUB) to look for abscess, stone, obstruction, or emphysematous changes.**
- **Obtain two sets of blood cultures before antibiotics — this is the only way to detect ESBL/MRSA organisms and escalate appropriately.**

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not rely on urine dipstick alone if patient has signs of sepsis** — start empiric antibiotics immediately.
- **Do not discharge a febrile patient with pyelonephritis on oral antibiotics without 24 h afebrile period** — risk of early relapse.
- **Do not forget to check gentamicin levels (trough and peak if using once-daily) and adjust dose or interval** — preventable nephrotoxicity.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Fever not settling after 48 h of oral antibiotics
  - Worsening flank pain, rigors, confusion, or reduced urine output
  - Unable to tolerate oral fluids or medication (vomiting)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 48–72 h for clinical check
  - Repeat urine culture 7 days after finishing antibiotics if recurrent infection or pregnancy
  - Outpatient CT KUB if first episode in male, recurrent, or complicated (arrange before discharge)

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥5** → review by med reg/ICU outreach; consider critical care transfer.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L after initial fluid bolus** → escalate to ICU for vasopressors and source control.
- **MAP <65 mmHg despite 1L crystalloid** → involve ICU; consider noradrenaline.
- **Anuria or acute renal failure (creatinine rise >100% from baseline) + flank pain/proven obstruction** → urgent urology for decompression (nephrostomy or stent).
